title: Easy v0 saturates the model — benchmark is the bottleneck
finding: Frozen Qwen3.5-4B scores F1 ~98-100 on v0 with recall 100%. No headroom to measure any SFT/CoT/OPD delta; differences would be lost in noise.
suggestions: Add hard families (counterfactual, intent-only, near-boundary, obfuscation) before training.

title: Regex is a clean high-precision / low-recall foil
finding: Regex baseline: P~95 R~30 F1~45. Catches secret_credential & infra_config (literal secrets) but is 0% on access_control / security_policy / vuln_incident.
suggestions: Keep regex as a deterministic pre-filter for literal-secret subcategories; rely on the model for semantic intent.

title: Counterfactual pairs are the entire difficulty signal
finding: v1 drops model F1 to 96.2. All errors are engineered boundaries: over-triggers on access REQUESTS (vs grants) and third-party breach NEWS (vs our incident). Counterfactual slice specificity ~58%.
suggestions: Train with CoT that explicitly reasons: is this ENACTED? is this US? before labeling.
Push counterfactual volume/variety for a steeper curve.

title: Model already robust to obfuscation and intent-only disclosure
finding: Negative result: obfuscation (base64/spaced secrets) and intent-only positives (no literal secret) both score 100%. These families add coverage but not difficulty.
suggestions: Do not over-invest in obfuscation/intent-only; concentrate hard-data effort on counterfactual + near-boundary.

title: Report specificity on all-negative slices (eval honesty)
finding: near_boundary is all-negative, so P/R/F1 are degenerate (F1=0 looked like failure). Its true performance is 94.4% specificity.
suggestions: Per-slice now shows support (pos count) + specificity; use specificity for negative-heavy slices.

title: Over-triggering is the dominant failure mode; new pair types isolate it
finding: v2 model: recall 100%, all 7 errors are false positives. The model fires on near-boundary negatives that share surface features with positives: the AWS docs EXAMPLE key (twice), access REQUESTS/proposals (not enacted), synthetic test PII (000-00-0000 fixtures), and third-party breach NEWS.
suggestions: Primary training objective should reduce false positives: teach 'is it real / enacted / us?' Reasoning (CoT) likely helps most here.
Pick a precision-favoring operating point for IT (FP fatigue) once we can tune a threshold.

title: Slice F1 is not comparable across versions when the slice content changes
finding: Model is unchanged (frozen Qwen3.5-4B) between v1 and v2, so every metric delta is due to DATA composition, not model skill. Counterfactual F1 'rose' 78.6->88.9 only because v2 added some easier pair types alongside harder ones. The Versions ΔF1 measures benchmark change here, not model improvement.
suggestions: When reading version diffs, hold the model fixed OR the data fixed — not both. Use frozen-model diffs to gauge benchmark difficulty; use fixed-data diffs to gauge training gains.

title: Harder counterfactuals open ~7pts of headroom; FP profile is crisp
finding: Frozen model F1 95.8->93.3 (CI 89-97), recall still 100%. The 12 FPs cluster: documented example keys (AWS docs example x4, Stripe test key), third-party breach NEWS x4, plus the new expired/dead key, revoke-proposal, synthetic test PII, and last-4 card. Regex precision also fell to 81% (it flags the example keys too).
suggestions: Two clean training targets remain: (a) example/placeholder secrets that look real, (b) third-party news vs our incident. CoT 'is it real / ours / enacted?' should hit both.

title: Rubric call: explicitly dead/revoked credentials labeled PASS
finding: Deliberate, documented choice (cf_expired_vs_live): a credential stated to be rotated-out/revoked grants nothing, so it's labeled negative; the live one is positive. The frozen model currently over-triggers on the dead key (fires on the AKIA shape regardless of 'dead now' context). This is a defensible-but-contestable boundary worth flagging to reviewers.
suggestions: If reviewers prefer treating any key-shaped string as sensitive, flip these labels and regenerate; the generator makes it a one-line change.

title: Real eval exposes a synthetic->real gap AND a labeling bug (CVE)
finding: Combined eval: synthetic F1 94.9 vs REAL F1 41.0. But it decomposes: 91 of 107 real false-positives are CVE advisories (security_news spec 2.2%). Excluding CVE, real F1=76.9 / spec=93.8 — the model transfers reasonably to real negatives (code 99%, ticket 98%, support 97% spec); residual gap is precision on PII prose (spec 74%).
suggestions: Don't read 'F1 41' as pure model failure — slice it. The model's real weakness is moderate over-triggering on PII prose, not collapse.

title: RUBRIC DECISION NEEDED: are third-party CVE advisories in or out of scope?
finding: I mapped CVE -> security_news -> NEGATIVE, but the IT policy lists 'vulnerability/incident details' as IN-SCOPE. The model flags 91/93 CVEs as TRIGGER — arguably agreeing with the policy, not my label. This single choice dominates the real headline metric. It also contradicts my synthetic cf_cve_advisory pair (CVE-about-unused-lib -> negative), so the rubric is currently inconsistent.
suggestions: Decide: (a) CVE advisories = POSITIVE (vulnerability details, in-scope) — recommended for a safety-first IT flag; reserve 'security_news' negative for pure commentary ('Company X breached, analysts say'); (b) keep negative because a PUBLISHED CVE isn't a sensitive disclosure; (c) drop CVE as ambiguous. Then realign synthetic cf_cve_advisory to match.

title: CVE dropped -> clean synthetic->real gap is ~25 F1 (94.9 -> 70.0), and genuine
finding: With CVE removed, real spec is healthy (93.5%); the gap is now real model behavior, not a label artifact. Two drivers: (1) over-triggering on casual-PII PROSE (pii_handling spec 71%) — names/emails in ordinary text we label negative; (2) lower RECALL on real positives (80% vs 100% synthetic) — real breach tickets / PII-handling are phrased unlike our templates, so the model misses some. Code/support/helpdesk real negatives are ~98-100% spec.
suggestions: Training target shifts: synthetic over-triggers (FP), real also UNDER-triggers (FN) on out-of-distribution phrasing. A small real slice in TRAIN should help recall; CoT should help PII-prose precision.
PII-prose spec is partly label ambiguity (is a name/email in prose 'PII handling'?) — tighten the rubric for low-sensitivity PII.

title: Casual register + typos expose typo-brittleness (and close part of the realism gap)
finding: Added a casual long-form family (Slack/Reddit PSAs, vents, rambling disclosures) and a noise injector (40% of rows, secret/number spans protected). New slice 'noisy': clean rows F1 95.6/spec 89.4 vs noisy F1 88.9/spec 74.3 — the model over-triggers ~15pts more on misspelled negatives (recall stays 100%). Casual positives (buried secrets/PII/access) were caught fine; the PSA-style casual negatives are a clean new hard negative.
suggestions: Train with noise augmentation (the generator does this for free) to harden against typos — likely lifts real-data specificity too, since real text is messy.
Keep noise OFF secret spans (already enforced) so regex + secret labels stay valid.

title: SFT solves the engineered weaknesses; fixes real under-triggering
finding: First SFT (LoRA r32, 2 epochs, synthetic+real, noise-augmented) vs frozen baseline, SAME held-out test (valid model-changed/data-fixed comparison). Synthetic F1 92.8->99.4, real F1 70->73.7. Counterfactual specificity 47.6->95.2 and noisy specificity 74->100 (noise aug worked, per f013). Real recall 80->100 (under-triggering on OOD phrasing fixed), recall now 100% everywhere (0 FN).
suggestions: Next: CoT SFT should target the remaining real PII-prose over-triggering (prose spec 55%). Also tighten the casual/low-sensitivity PII rubric — some of those 'FPs' are debatable labels.
Watch for overfitting to the label format (train NLL ~0.005); CoT and OPD are the levers for the harder real precision.
